content: Encryption preserves the confidentiality of online messages by transforming plaintext into ciphertext using cryptographic algorithms keyed by secret values known only to authorized parties. In practice, end-to-end encryption protocols leverage a combination of asymmetric key exchange (e.g., Diffie-Hellman or elliptic-curve variants) to establish shared symmetric session keys, which then encrypt message payloads with algorithms like AES. This layered approach ensures that even intermediaries—including servers and network nodes—cannot decrypt the contents. Importantly, modern protocols incorporate forward secrecy to limit exposure from future key compromises and integrate authentication to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks. While encryption effectively obfuscates message content, it does not inherently conceal metadata such as message timing, size, or participants, which can leak sensitive information unless addressed by additional mechanisms like onion routing. Thus, encryption’s core contribution to privacy lies in cryptographically enforced confidentiality guarantees that render intercepted data computationally infeasible to decipher without the appropriate keys.
